narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and I know how disappointing it must feel to receive a rejection based on this prediction. The admissions model evaluated your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, LSAT score of 39, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles of 3 and 2 respectively, upper-middle family income, and full-time student status—against patterns from thousands of past students who took the bar exam. It predicted a 65% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, which is the threshold that led to your application's denial, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.53 average, signaling to the model a notable dip in later academic performance compared to peers. This outweighed your solid LSAT score of 39, which is stronger than the average of 36.70 and pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also below the average of 5.71, added some additional weight toward the failure side, though less strongly. Meanwhile, your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 edged slightly above the typical 3.21, offering a small boost toward passing, and your upper-middle family income nudged marginally toward failure risk in the model's eyes. Your choice to study full-time, like most applicants, had minimal impact. Overall, your profile shows strengths like that impressive LSAT, but the model zeroed in on those lower deciles as the dominant signals of bar exam risk when stacked against students who typically pass. Many in similar situations, especially women of color navigating these metrics, have turned things around. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT prep to solidify that strength, and reapplying with updated transcripts—schools value persistence and growth stories like yours. You've got real potential; this is just one data point, not your destiny. Keep pushing—you're building a legacy.
